14 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The right ERP cannot be identified until leadership defines the Business Intent it must serve.Every ERP roadmap eventually arrives at the same debate.

• Stay or migrate?

• Which ERP platform?

• Suite or best-of-breed?

• Standardize or customize?

• Set a global standard or allow local variation?

• Cloud or on-premise?

• Embedded AI or independent AI?
Executives, analysts, vendors, and consultants spend thousands of hours debating the merits of each option.
The discussions are often serious. The analysis is detailed. The decisions carry enormous financial and operational consequences.
Yet there is a question that is almost never given the attention it deserves:
Right for what?
Right for what business outcome?
Right for what future operating model?
Right for what strategic priority?
Right for what Conditions of Success?
Right for what evidence of value?
The absence of that question may explain why so many ERP transformations deliver a new platform without delivering the outcomes that justified the investment.
The Industry’s Favorite Debate
ERP conversations tend to follow a familiar pattern.
Leadership teams debate technology roadmaps, vendor strategies, AI announcements, cloud migrations, technical debt, support models, integration architectures, licensing arrangements, customization policies, and operating costs.
They consider whether to adopt a single global template or permit local variations. They debate standard functionality versus customization, suite integration versus best-of-breed specialization, and embedded AI versus independent AI capabilities.
These are legitimate considerations.
Technology matters. Architecture matters. Cost matters. Security matters. Vendor viability matters. The ability to support and evolve the solution matters.
The problem is not that organizations ask these questions.
The problem is the sequence in which they ask them.
The discussion often begins with the technology and only later attempts to connect the preferred technology to a business rationale.
The CIO may believe the organization should move to a modern cloud platform.
The finance team may want stronger planning, consolidation, and reporting capabilities.
Operations may want improved supply-chain visibility.
IT may want to reduce technical debt and simplify integrations.
Regional leaders may want to preserve local practices.
A software vendor may present a compelling innovation roadmap.
An implementation partner may recommend adopting industry best practices.
Every argument may sound reasonable. Yet unless the organization has established an authoritative definition of what the Transformation Program is meant to accomplish, the alternatives cannot be evaluated against a common purpose.
The organization may know what each platform offers.
It may still not know what the business actually requires.
Organizations often spend more time discussing where the system should run than why the transformation exists.
The Most Important Question Nobody Asks
Imagine a board meeting.
The CIO says:
“We should migrate to Fusion.”
A consulting partner says:
“We should move to S/4HANA.”
A vendor says:
“We need a cloud-first strategy.”
A business executive says:
“We need AI embedded across finance and operations.”
At that moment, someone should ask:
“What business outcome are we trying to achieve?”
Not:

• Which platform is better?

• Which vendor has more AI?

• Which architecture is more modern?

• Which solution has the most features?

• Which vendor has the strongest roadmap?
But:

• What specific business outcome must improve?

• How much improvement is required?

• What must operate differently to produce that improvement?

• What Conditions of Success must be satisfied?

• How will we know whether the outcome was achieved?

• What evidence will leadership require before accepting a claim of success?
Until those questions are answered, no technology recommendation can be objectively evaluated.
A platform can be modern without being right for the business.
A cloud architecture can be scalable without addressing the organization’s most important constraints.
An AI-enabled solution can be innovative without improving an outcome leadership values.
A vendor can demonstrate hundreds of capabilities without proving that the solution supports the Business Intent the Sponsor authorized.
“Right” is not an inherent property of a software platform.
A solution is only right in relation to a clearly defined purpose.
Technology Decisions Are Not Business Strategies
One reason ERP discussions become confused is that Business Strategy, Business Intent, business capabilities, and technology strategy are frequently treated as interchangeable.
They are not.
Business Strategy
Business Strategy defines what the organization is trying to accomplish.
Examples might include:

• Reduce order-to-cash cycle time by 50 percent

• Improve inventory turns

• Accelerate acquisition integration

• Increase service profitability

• Improve working capital performance

• Support expansion into new markets
These strategic objectives provide direction, but they do not yet define how the organization must operate to achieve them.
Business Intent
Business Intent translates strategic direction into an explicit definition of what the organization intends to operationalize.
It may establish:

• Intended Business Outcomes

• Conditions of Success

• Decision boundaries

• Accountability requirements

• Exception rules

• Evidence requirements

• Governance boundaries

• Business rules that must remain authoritative
This is the layer that turns broad strategic ambition into something the organization can govern, evaluate, operationalize, and validate.
Business Capability Strategy
Business Capability Strategy identifies how the organization must become more capable.
Examples might include:

• Better planning

• Faster forecasting

• Improved pricing governance

• Enhanced customer visibility

• More consistent approval authority

• Stronger acquisition integration capabilities
These capabilities describe what the organization must be able to do differently or better.
Technology Strategy
Technology Strategy determines how systems will support the required capabilities and Business Intent.
The technology may include:

• ERP

• CRM

• Analytics

• AI

• Automation

• Workflow platforms

• Data services

• Integration architecture
Most transformations invert this sequence.
They begin with technology and work backward.
A preferred solution is identified. A business case is constructed. Requirements are gathered. The organization then tries to connect the solution to the outcomes leadership expects.
The stronger sequence begins with Business Strategy, defines Business Intent, identifies the capabilities required, and only then determines which technology is best suited to support them.
Technology should follow Business Intent.
Business Intent should not be reconstructed from the technology an organization has already decided to buy.
Why “Stay or Migrate?” Is Usually the Wrong Question
Consider an organization running JD Edwards.
The conventional question is:
“Should we stay on JD Edwards?”
That framing immediately pulls the conversation toward support timelines, hosting models, available skills, technical debt, integrations, licensing costs, cloud alternatives, and vendor roadmaps.
Those issues matter, but the better question is:
“Can JD Edwards support the outcomes we need?”
That question forces the organization to define the outcomes before evaluating the platform.
Likewise, the question is not:
“Should we migrate to SAP?”
The better question is:
“Will SAP materially improve our ability to achieve our intended outcomes?”
The difference is substantial.
“Should we migrate?” invites a debate about products.
“Can the current platform support the outcomes we need?” invites a debate about the business.
That conversation can address:

• Growth

• Profitability

• Speed

• Customer service

• Compliance

• Operational performance

• Working capital

• Decision quality

• Organizational scalability

• Acquisition readiness
Once the intended outcomes and operating requirements are explicit, staying may prove to be the most responsible decision.
The existing ERP may be capable of supporting the future operating model with targeted modernization, improved data, better integrations, revised processes, or selected extensions.
In another organization, the analysis may demonstrate that the current platform cannot support the intended outcomes without unacceptable complexity, cost, or constraint. Migration may then be justified.
In both cases, the answer is derived from business purpose rather than technology preference.
This is not an argument for staying.
It is not an argument for migrating.
It is an argument for establishing what the platform must enable before deciding whether any platform is right.
Standardize or Customize?
Once an organization selects a platform, another familiar debate begins.
Should the business adopt the standard processes embedded in the software, or should the software be customized around the way the organization operates?
Standardization can reduce complexity, simplify support, improve consistency, and make future upgrades easier.
Customization can preserve differentiated capabilities, accommodate important operating requirements, and support practices that create genuine competitive value.
Neither approach is inherently correct.
A standard process is not automatically a better process simply because a software vendor designed it.
A customization is not automatically valuable simply because the organization has relied on it for years.
The right question is:
“Which elements of the operating model must remain distinctive to achieve our Business Outcomes, and which should become standard?”
Without that distinction, organizations tend to fall into one of two extremes.
They either reproduce almost every historical practice in the new system, carrying unnecessary complexity into the future, or they accept standard functionality indiscriminately, removing practices that may be essential to the organization’s strategy, controls, customers, or operating model.
Business Intent provides the reference needed to distinguish valuable differentiation from inherited complexity.
Preserve Legacy Customizations or Redesign for the Future?
A migration often creates pressure to decide whether legacy customizations should be carried into the new platform.
Some customizations may represent essential business rules, controls, customer commitments, accountability requirements, or differentiated capabilities.
Some may also support obligations embedded in long-term customer, supplier, or partner contracts. A customization that operationalizes contractual pricing, service levels, fulfillment commitments, billing arrangements, reporting obligations, or other negotiated terms cannot be evaluated solely as a technical artifact. Before changing or retiring it, the organization must determine which contractual obligations depend on it and whether those obligations must be preserved, renegotiated, or supported differently in the future solution.
Others may reflect:

• Historical system limitations

• Old organizational structures

• Temporary workarounds

• Inconsistent local practices

• Obsolete policies

• Unexamined preferences

• Decisions whose original rationale has been lost
The assessment should also quantify how frequently each customization is actually used, by whom, for which transactions, in which business units or regions, and under what conditions. A customization described as business-critical may support only a small number of transactions, a narrow user group, or an exception that rarely occurs. Low usage does not automatically mean low value, especially when the customization supports a material control, contractual obligation, regulatory requirement, or high-cons
